title: # 23: Add centralized `config.js` and backend architecture guidelines
body:
# PR: Add centralized `config.js` and backend architecture guidelines

## Description

This PR introduces:

- A centralized `config.js` file for managing environment variables and reusable backend configurations.
- A `backend-rules.md` documentation file that defines backend folder responsibilities, coding standards, architecture conventions, validation rules, and error handling practices.

The goal of this PR is to improve maintainability, scalability, and contributor onboarding by standardizing backend structure and configuration management.

---

# Changes Made

## Added Centralized Configuration

Created:

```bash
src/config/config.js
```

### Responsibilities

- Loads environment variables
- Exports reusable config values
- Centralizes application configuration
- Reduces direct usage of `process.env`

### Example

```js
import dotenv from "dotenv";

dotenv.config();

export const config = {
  port: process.env.PORT || 3000,
  mongoURI: process.env.MONGO_URI,
  jwtSecret: process.env.JWT_SECRET,
  nodeEnv: process.env.NODE_ENV || "development",
};
```

---

## Added Backend Rules Documentation

Created:

```bash
docs/backend-rules.md
```

### Includes

- Folder responsibilities
- Coding standards
- Controller/service/DAO separation
- Validation guidelines
- Error handling conventions
- API response standards

---

# Backend Architecture Standardized

## Folder Responsibilities

| Folder | Responsibility |
|--------|----------------|
| config/ | Environment variables and app configuration |
| controllers/ | Request and response handling |
| services/ | Business logic |
| dao/ | Database access logic |
| middleware/ | Express middlewares |
| models/ | Database schemas/models |
| routes/ | API route definitions |
| validation/ | Request validation |
| utils/ | Shared helper utilities |

---

# Benefits

- Improves code maintainability
- Makes configuration management centralized
- Establishes backend development standards
- Makes onboarding easier for contributors
- Encourages clean architecture practices
- Reduces duplicated configuration logic

---

# Future Improvements

- Add environment variable validation
- Add typed configuration support
- Add API documentation standards
- Add testing conventions documentation

---

# Checklist

- [x] Added centralized configuration file
- [x] Added backend rules documentation
- [x] Followed existing project structure
- [x] Improved maintainability
- [x] Added scalable backend conventions

title: feat(backend): add mongodb connection and base models
body:
What does this PR do?

Adds MongoDB support via Mongoose, including connection helper.
Adds User and UploadHistory models.
Updates backend env docs with [MONGO_URI]

Which Stage does this belong to?
Stage 4: Backend & Authentication (Node.js)

Why is this change needed?
Phase 3 requires user sessions and upload history, so DB foundation is needed.

title: Improve loading feedback and route transition UX
body:
This PR improves loading feedback and async UX handling across the application.

### Changes Made

* improved reusable loading spinner system
* added inline button loading indicators
* integrated loading feedback into async workflows
* cleaned duplicate spinner styles/components
* added lightweight route-transition loading using React Suspense and lazy loading
* prevented spinner flickering during fast route changes
* preserved fast navigation experience without introducing artificial delays

### Testing

* verified route navigation across pages
* tested Upload, Home, and Admin routes
* confirmed no console/build errors
* verified loading states behave correctly during slower transitions
* ensured fast transitions remain smooth without unnecessary spinner flicker

Addresses loading feedback improvements discussed in #14

repo.description: Working on this issue under GSSoC'26.

title: centralized error handeling
body:
Implement Centralized Error Handling & Standard API Responses

Overview

This PR introduces a centralized error handling system for the backend to improve:

  • API consistency

  • maintainability

  • debugging

  • scalability

  • frontend integration

The implementation removes repetitive error handling logic from controllers and introduces a standardized architecture for handling application errors.

Changes Made

Added Global Error Middleware

Added a centralized Express error middleware:

src/middleware/error.middleware.js

This middleware now handles all application errors in one place and returns standardized API responses.

Added Custom Error Classes

Introduced reusable custom error classes:

src/utils/errors/
├── AppError.js
├── ValidationError.js
├── AuthError.js
└── NotFoundError.js

Purpose

Error Class | Responsibility -- | -- AppError | Base custom error class ValidationError | Validation-related errors AuthError | Authentication & authorization errors NotFoundError | Resource not found errors

Added Async Error Wrapper

Added reusable async wrapper utility:

src/utils/catchAsync.js

This removes the need for repetitive try/catch blocks inside controllers.

Example Usage

Before

const createUser = async (req, res) => {
   try {
      const user = await userService.createUser(req.body);

      res.status(201).json({
         success: true,
         data: user
      });

   } catch(err) {
      res.status(500).json({
         success: false,
         message: err.message
      });
   }
};

After

const catchAsync = require("../utils/catchAsync");

const createUser = catchAsync(async (req, res) => {

   const user = await userService.createUser(req.body);

   res.status(201).json({
      success: true,
      data: user
   });
});

Standardized Error Response Format

All API errors now follow a consistent structure:

{
   "success": false,
   "message": "Validation Failed"
}

This improves frontend predictability and simplifies error handling on the client side.

Request Lifecycle

Request
   ↓
Route
   ↓
Validation
   ↓
Controller
   ↓
Service
   ↓
DAO
   ↓
Database
   ↓
Error Thrown (if any)
   ↓
Global Error Middleware
   ↓
Standardized API Response

Benefits

Cleaner Controllers

Controllers are now focused only on request handling and business flow.

Reduced Code Duplication

No need to repeatedly write:

  • try/catch blocks

  • response formatting logic

Better Scalability

The architecture is now more maintainable as the project grows:

  • authentication

  • AI processing

  • image uploads

  • payments

  • background jobs

Improved Debugging

All errors pass through one centralized location, making:

  • logging

  • monitoring

  • debugging

much easier.

Future Ready

This structure allows easy future integration with:

  • Winston/Pino logging

  • Sentry

  • structured logs

  • request tracing

  • production-safe stack traces

Notes

This PR focuses only on backend error handling architecture and response standardization.

No major feature-level changes were introduced.

Closes #

title: Backend Folder Structure Optimization
body:
Backend Folder Structure Optimization

Overview

This PR reorganizes and standardizes the backend folder structure to improve:

  • maintainability

  • scalability

  • separation of concerns

  • contributor experience

Since the project is currently in an early stage, introducing a cleaner architecture now will make future development significantly easier.

Changes Made

Added / Organized Core Backend Structure

Backend/
│
├── docs/
│
├── src/
│   ├── config/
│   ├── controllers/
│   ├── dao/
│   ├── middleware/
│   ├── models/
│   ├── routes/
│   ├── service/
│   ├── utils/
│   ├── validation/
│   └── app.js
│
├── server.js
├── package.json
└── README.md

Purpose of Each Directory

| Folder | Responsibility | |--------|----------------| | `config/` | Environment variables, database configuration, and app-level configuration | | `controllers/` | Handles incoming requests and sends responses | | `dao/` | Database access layer and query logic | | `middleware/` | Express middlewares such as authentication, validation, and error handling | | `models/` | Database schemas and models | | `routes/` | API route definitions and route grouping | | `service/` | Business logic and application-level operations | | `utils/` | Shared helper functions and reusable utilities | | `validation/` | Request validation schemas and validation logic |

Benefits

Better Separation of Concerns

Each layer now has a dedicated responsibility:

  • Controllers → request handling

  • Services → business logic

  • DAO → database operations

This reduces coupling and improves maintainability.

Easier Scalability

This structure is future-ready for:

  • authentication

  • AI processing pipelines

  • payment integration

  • queue systems

  • background workers

  • analytics

  • cloud storage

Improved Contributor Experience

New contributors can now quickly understand:

  • where business logic belongs

  • where database logic exists

  • where validations are handled

  • how the request lifecycle flows

Cleaner Long-Term Architecture

This structure also makes future additions easier:

  • testing setup

  • modularization

  • microservice migration

  • logging systems

  • centralized error handling

Notes

This PR mainly focuses on backend architecture organization and does not introduce major feature-level changes.

Future improvements can now be built on top of this cleaner structure.

Closes #

title: feat: add proper empty state handling for editor and print preview pages
body:
## Summary

With reference to issue #3, this PR improves the user experience for first-time visitors by adding proper empty state handling for the Editor and Print Preview pages.

Previously, when users were navigating directly to these routes without uploading a photo, the page appeared blank reducing user experience.

## Changes Made

### Added Reusable Empty State Component

* Created a reusable `EmptyState` component with:

  * Helpful illustration/icon
  * User guidance message
  * Clear CTA button for navigation

### Editor Page Improvements

* Replaced forced redirect behavior with a proper empty state UI
* Users now see:

  * “No photo selected yet”
  * Guidance to upload a photo before editing

### Print Preview Page Improvements

* Replaced redirect fallback with informative empty state
* Users now receive clear instructions when no processed image is available

### UX Improvements

* Improved navigation clarity for new users
* Prevented confusing blank/redirect experiences
* Maintained existing routing flow without affecting current upload logic

## Why This Change?

The AI processing and print generation pipeline is still under active development. Until the complete workflow is implemented, proper empty state handling provides a significantly better user experience and prevents broken-looking pages.

## Future Scope

The following enhancements can be added later once processing is fully integrated:

* Print preview zoom controls
* PNG/PDF download support
* Orientation selection
* Advanced A4 print styling
* Sheet export improvements

title: feat(homepage): add AI workflow showcase section
body: ## What does this PR do? Currently the homepage had basic sections like the hero section, how it works, features and CTA sections. But in the hero section too we just show a skeleton of what we actually do with this project. So in this PR I have added a new section which shows a sample image and the generated sheet ready to print. ## Which Stage does this belong to? Stage 2: UI/UX Enhancements ## Why is this change needed? This closes #4 The user will now see the results our platform produces even before using it and will be eager to try on their own image. ## Screenshots (Mandatory if UI change) Before: After: ## Checklist - [x] Tested locally and in browser. - [x] No console errors or warnings. - [x] Mobile responsive (if UI). - [x] Code is commented where needed. - [x] Commit messages follow the Conventional Commits standard.
